That's true! There are many factors that contribute to weight gain or loss, including genetics, metabolism, body composition, diet quality, physical activity level, and overall lifestyle. While some people may have a faster metabolism or be more efficient at burning calories, others may have a slower metabolism or store fat more easily.

It's also important to note that eating a lot doesn't necessarily mean you'll gain weight. If you're consuming high-quality foods with plenty of nutrients, fiber, and water, your body will be able to process those calories efficiently. Additionally, regular exercise and other forms of physical activity can help burn off excess energy and support weight management.

Rather than focusing on what others can do, try to focus on making healthy choices for yourself. Eat a balanced diet with plenty of fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy fats. Stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water throughout the day. Aim for 150 minutes of moderate-intensity exercise or 75 minutes of vigorous-intensity exercise per week. And get enough sleep each night to help regulate your appetite hormones and support weight regulation.

Remember, everyone's body is different, and it's okay if you don't look like someone else. Focus on being the best version of yourself, and celebrate your unique strengths
